Suggest a new scanning probe measurement.answer: Scanning Localized Coherent X-ray Source Microscopy (SLCXSM): a nano-undulator integrated at the probe apex produces a confined coherent soft-X-ray beam that interacts with the surface directly beneath the tip; rastering and detecting the resulting near-field x-ray fluorescence/absorption yields element-specific chemical and electronic maps with nanometre resolution.

Scanning Chemical Recombination Energy Microscopy (SCREM): a functionalized probe tip bearing metastable radicals rasters the surface while an integrated nanocalorimeter records the picojoule heat pulses released when radicals recombine at reactive sites, yielding nanometre-resolution maps of surface chemical reactivity.

Scanning Josephson Photon-Emission Microscopy (SJPEM): a superconducting tip forms a local Josephson junction with the surface and, while rastering, the spatially resolved spectrum and rate of AC-Josephson microwave photons emitted across the junction are detected, yielding nanometre-resolution maps of the local superconducting gap, phase coherence and electromagnetic impedance.

Scanning Quantum Gravimetry Microscopy (SQGM): a probe tip hosting a nanometre-scale atom-interferometric gravimeter is rastered above a surface; by reading the phase shift of the confined ultracold atoms’ free-fall matter waves, the tool maps local variations in the gravitational potential—hence subsurface mass density—with nanometre spatial resolution.

Scanning Aharonov–Bohm Phase Microscopy (SABPM): a nano-interferometric loop at the probe apex guides coherent electrons and reads their local vector-potential–induced phase shift while rastering, thereby imaging nanoscale distributions of orbital currents and electromagnetic potentials without requiring direct magnetic field penetration.

Scanning Low-Energy Neutron Probe Microscopy (SLENPM): a nanofabricated tip that emits and detects tightly collimated slow neutrons, allowing raster-scanned imaging of local nuclear composition and magnetic textures with nanometre resolution.

Provide an explanation for the role of \.answer: So-called “junk” DNA, although it does not code for proteins, contains regulatory and structural sequences and non-coding RNAs that control when and where genes are switched on, maintain chromosome architecture, and provide raw material for evolution.

“Junk” DNA includes non-coding sequences that guide gene expression, organize chromatin, protect chromosome ends, and serve as evolutionary reservoirs for new functions, showing it is biologically useful rather than useless.

DNA once labeled “junk” performs essential non-coding tasks: it houses enhancers, silencers, introns, telomere and centromere sequences, and templates for regulatory RNAs, thereby governing gene activity, preserving chromosome stability, and offering sequence diversity for evolutionary innovation.

Propose a biological experiment to test.answer: Establish cryogenic reactors filled with liquid methane-ethane (94 K, 1.5 bar), seed them with simple organics detected on Titan (acetylene, hydrogen cyanide, acrylonitrile, CH₄-soluble phosphates) and bubble in reductive gases (H₂, NH₃). Apply cycling energy sources that would exist on Titan—UV photons, electrical discharges and periodic temperature fluctuations. Continuously monitor the reactors, and matched aqueous controls, for (1) spontaneous formation of stable amphiphilic “azotosome” vesicles, (2) concentration-dependent polymerization into information-bearing nitrile or peptide-like chains, and (3) emergence of autocatalytic reaction networks that replicate vesicle-bound polymers faster than controls. Demonstrating sustained vesicle formation and autocatalytic replication exclusively in the methane-ethane reactors would support the hypothesis that life could originate in a non-aqueous solvent.

Construct sealed cryostats maintained at 200 K containing liquid anhydrous ammonia seeded with a defined mixture of prebiotically plausible solutes—hydrogen cyanide, acetaldehyde, phosphate, and ammonia-soluble long-chain nitriles. 1) Pulse the reactors with UV photons and electric sparks to simulate Jovian-moon surface energy. 2) Impose daily −20 K temperature oscillations to create freeze–melt concentration cycles unique to liquid NH₃. 3) In parallel aqueous controls, apply identical energy and thermal regimes. Continuously sample both sets for: a) formation of stable amphiphilic assemblies (“ammoniosomes”) that exclude solvent; b) condensation of nucleobase-nitrile and peptide bonds tracked by mass spectrometry; c) amplification of any autocatalytic network by isotopically labeling one precursor and following its enrichment in products over successive cycles. Observation of vesicle-encapsulated polymers exhibiting self-enhancing synthesis exclusively in the ammonia reactors would support the hypothesis that life can originate in a non-aqueous solvent.

Propose a synthetic chromosome design th.answer: A linear ~1 Mb synthetic chromosome (“RadGuard-1”) carrying: (1) tandemly-arrayed, codon-optimized human RAD51, RAD52, XRCC6/Ku70-Ku80 and DNA-PKcs genes under radiation-inducible promoters for rapid homologous-recombination and NHEJ repair; (2) bacterial photolyase and Deinococcus radiodurans PprA genes for direct UV/DSB repair; (3) mitochondrial Mn-superoxide dismutase, catalase and peroxiredoxin clusters driven by constitutive promoters to quench ROS; (4) a synthetic, GC-rich “chromatin-sink” domain with scaffold/matrix attachment regions to absorb double-strand breaks away from essential chromosomes. RadGuard-1 is flanked by telomere and centromere sequences compatible with the host, replicates once per cycle, and is transcriptionally insulated to minimize metabolic burden, collectively increasing cellular resistance to ionizing radiation.

Design a 500 kb, high-copy, circular neo-chromosome (“RadiShield-π”) that autonomously replicates using an engineered centromere/origin pair and carries:
1. A melanin-biosynthesis operon (tyrosinase, laccase, DHN-pathway enzymes) under a radiation-responsive promoter to create an intracellular pigment screen that absorbs ionizing and UV radiation.
2. A Deinococcus radiodurans DNA-repair cluster (recA, recO, recR, pprA, gyrA) flanked by synthetic riboswitches that trigger strong expression only after double-strand-break (DSB) sensing.
3. A CRISPR-guided “search-and-patch” cassette: nuclease-dead Cas9 fused to Rad52 that is programmed with a library of DSB-hotspot guide RNAs, accelerating homologous recombination at break sites.
4. An antioxidant/ROS-quenching module (Mn-superoxide dismutase, catalase, peroxiredoxin, and trehalose-synthesis genes) driven by a constitutive low-level promoter.
5. A GC-rich, heterochromatin-forming “sacrificial sponge” region (~80 kb) with matrix-attachment repeats that preferentially attracts radiation-induced breaks away from essential host chromosomes.
The neo-chromosome is transcriptionally insulated, selectable via an auxotrophy marker, and yields host cells that survive >10-fold higher γ-ray doses than wild type.

Engineer a 600 kb linear accessory chromosome (“Shield-ChrT”) bearing: (1) a codon-optimized tardigrade Dsup gene fused to a nuclear-localization signal and expressed from a constitutive, low-level promoter to coat host chromatin and physically attenuate radiation tracks; (2) an archaeal photolyase-oxidoreductase operon under a light-inducible promoter for rapid reversal of UV- and ionizing-radiation lesions; (3) a clustered ROS-detoxification array (Mn-SOD, catalase, glutathione reductase) driven by a mild constitutive promoter; and (4) a 150 kb GC-rich “sacrificial repeat zone” containing scaffold-attachment motifs that preferentially absorbs double-strand breaks, sparing essential chromosomes. Telomere and centromere elements matched to the host ensure stable, once-per-cycle replication with minimal fitness cost, resulting in markedly enhanced cellular survival after γ- or UV-irradiation.

Propose a completely different genetic c.answer: A peptide-nucleic-acid (PNA) system: genetic information is stored in a linear polymer whose backbone is repeating N-(2-aminoethyl)-glycine units (a neutral peptide chain) rather than sugar–phosphate; side-chain nucleobase analogs (A, T, C, G or others) hydrogen-bond to complementary bases, allowing templated self-replication and transcription by peptide ligase/transferase enzymes, thereby supporting heredity and evolution without DNA/RNA.

Silicone nucleic acid (SiNA): a genetic polymer whose backbone is a flexible polysiloxane chain (-Si–O-Si-) instead of sugar–phosphate; each silicon carries one of four organic side-groups (S¹, S², S³, S⁴) that act as nucleobase analogues forming specific hydrogen-bond pairs (S¹↔S³, S²↔S⁴). Complementary SiNA strands zip into a double helix stabilized by base pairing and hydrophobic stacking of the side-groups. An enzyme suite of siloxane polymerases and ligases mediates templated replication and transcription into functional silicoproteins, enabling heredity, mutation and evolution without any DNA/RNA chemistry.

A poly-thiophosphate genetic system (TPG): information is stored in a linear backbone of repeating thiophosphate groups (–P(=S)–O–CH₂–CH₂–) rather than sugar-phosphate; four distinct heterocyclic side-chains—imidazolyl (I), thiazolyl (T), pyridyl (P) and oxazolyl (O)—are attached via sulfur linkages and pair specifically through S··N hydrogen bonds (I↔O and T↔P). Complementary strands form a stable double helix, and specialized thiophosphate polymerases mediate template-directed synthesis by reversible sulfur-condensation, allowing faithful replication, transcription into thiophosphate-linked peptides, mutation, and evolution without any DNA or RNA.

Describe a synthetic genetic circuit tha.answer: A synthetic circuit could integrate a plant cryptochrome gene (Cry1) that forms magnetic-field–sensitive radical pairs with a split transcription factor: Cry1 is fused to one half of a dimerization-dependent activator (e.g., VP16-bZIP), while its redox partner protein MagR is fused to the complementary half. When weak geomagnetic-scale fields alter the singlet-triplet ratio of the Cry1/MagR radical pair, dimerization efficiency changes, modulating transcription from a downstream promoter that drives GFP and a quorum-sensing autoinducer synthase. The autoinducer diffuses, is re-imported, and through a LuxR-controlled positive feedback loop amplifies the small quantum-dependent signal into a population-wide fluorescent output, thus acting as a biological quantum sensor.

Engineer E. coli to express the flavin-binding BLUF photoreceptor PixD fused to one fragment of a split T7 RNA polymerase and its binding partner PixE fused to the complementary fragment. Upon blue-light excitation PixD forms a flavin radical pair whose singlet↔triplet interconversion rate is quantum-coherently perturbed by weak radio-frequency or geomagnetic fields. Field-dependent spin chemistry changes PixD–PixE association kinetics, altering reconstitution frequency of active T7 polymerase. The polymerase transcribes a T7-promoter-driven luxCDABE cassette, producing bioluminescence proportional to the quantum-modulated radical-pair dynamics, thereby creating a genetic circuit that functions as a biological quantum sensor.

Describe a synthetic genetic circuit tha.answer: Design a light-gated, ROS-amplified circuit in E. coli: express the flavin-binding photoreceptor miniSOG fused to bacterial ferredoxin (Fd). Blue-light excitation generates an FAD–Fd radical pair whose singlet↔triplet interconversion—and thus singlet-oxygen/H₂O₂ yield—is altered by weak magnetic or radio-frequency fields, a quantum‐coherent effect. H₂O₂ oxidises OxyR, which then activates a PoxyS promoter driving (i) sfGFP for read-out and (ii) NADH oxidase (nox) that recycles OxyR to its reduced state, sharpening temporal resolution. The field-dependent radical-pair chemistry is therefore converted into quantifiable fluorescence, creating a synthetic genetic circuit that operates as a biological quantum sensor.

Construct an E. coli circuit based on the LOV2 photoreceptor: (1) an FMN-binding LOV2 domain is fused to the N-terminal half of a split diguanylate cyclase (DGCN), while its interaction partner Zdk1 is fused to the C-terminal half (DGCC). (2) Blue-light excitation creates an FMN‒tryptophan radical pair in LOV2 whose singlet↔triplet interconversion rate is quantum-coherently shifted by weak magnetic or RF fields; this alters the lifetime of LOV2’s Jα-helix unfolding and hence the frequency of LOV2–Zdk1 binding. (3) Field-dependent reconstitution of active DGC modulates intracellular c-di-GMP. (4) A c-di-GMP riboswitch placed upstream of sfGFP converts the small, quantum-modulated c-di-GMP change into easily quantifiable fluorescence. Thus the engineered LOV2–DGC–riboswitch module functions as a biological quantum sensor that reports weak magnetic fields via spin-chemistry-controlled gene expression.

Engineer E. coli to express UV-A–activated DNA photolyase (PhrB) fused to one half of a split AraC activator, while its FAD-reducing partner protein (TrxA) is fused to the complementary half. Illumination creates an FAD•⁻/TrpH•⁺ radical pair in PhrB; weak magnetic or RF fields quantum-coherently alter the singlet–triplet interconversion, changing semiquinone lifetime and thus the probability that PhrB binds a synthetic cyclobutane-pyrimidine dimer (CPD) site engineered just upstream of a PBAD promoter. Field-dependent binding brings the two AraC halves together, reconstituting an active transcription factor that drives GFP expression. The circuit therefore transduces magnetically perturbed radical-pair chemistry in photolyase into quantifiable fluorescence, functioning as a biological quantum sensor.

Suggest a plausible pathway for the evol.answer: On a cold, high-pressure exoplanet with scarce carbon but abundant silicon, hydrogen, and nitrogen, volcanism releases SiH₄ (silane) into an ammonia-rich ocean. Lightning and UV radiation drive silane to polymerize into stable organosilicon chains (analogous to hydrocarbons) that remain flexible at ~120 K because the solvent is liquid NH₃. Mineral templates of clays catalyze the formation of cyclic polysilanes that can store energy by reversible Si-N and Si-H bond rearrangements. Copies of these molecules accumulate; those that self-assemble with inorganic phosphates form membranous SiO-SiN vesicles, creating the first protocells. Inside, catalytic polysilazanes evolve the ability to use geothermal redox gradients (Fe³⁺/Fe²⁺) to drive endergonic Si-H→Si-O substitutions, releasing free ammonia and generating chemical “ATP” analogs (poly-silanol phosphates). Competition for limited silane selects protocells with tighter, more selective siloxane membranes and error-correcting silane replicases. Over time, networks of polysilane-polysilazane reactions diversify into a silicon-based metabolism and genetics, giving rise to fully autotrophic, non-carbon, silicon-nitrogen life adapted to an ammonia ocean world.

On a tidally-heated, waterless exoplanet rich in molten sodium-potassium eutectic and exposed to dense SO₂ atmosphere, surface cooling solidifies thin films of liquid metal alloy beneath a crust of Na₂S glass. Solar UV splits SO₂, releasing reactive S and O that dissolve in the alloy, forming self-assembling Na-K-S-O ionic clusters whose polar shells exclude lighter metal atoms, creating compartmentalized “metallovesicles.” Thermal convection cycles these vesicles through redox gradients between sulfide-rich depths and oxide-rich surface, driving oscillating Na⁺/K⁺ charge separations that store free energy as electric double layers. Certain cluster motifs (alternating Na₆S and K₆O octahedra) catalyze their own templated growth by attracting matching ions; replication errors yield diversity. Vesicles that embed mixed-valence polysulfide chains gain superior electron conduction, enhancing energy capture and replication speed, and are selected. Progressive elaboration of conduction networks evolves into a metal-ionic metabolism using flowing electrons instead of carbon chemistry, eventually giving rise to fully self-sustaining, electrically coded, sodium-potassium-sulfur life in a liquid-metal ocean world.

On a temperate exoplanet poor in carbon but rich in boron-bearing silicates and a dense N₂-H₂ atmosphere, continual lightning reduces atmospheric N₂ and surface borates to volatile aminoboranes (H₂BNH₂) and boranes (BₙHₙ). In shallow, −80 °C liquid-NH₃ lakes these species polymerize into flexible B-N chains and rings—polyborazylene and borazine—whose alternating electron-rich N and electron-poor B sites foster hydrogen-bonded base-pairing. Mineral surfaces template complementary pairing of two borazine strands, enabling error-prone, autocatalytic replication. Amphiphilic borazine trimers capped with fluoroborate groups segregate from the solvent and self-assemble into bilayer vesicles, trapping replicating B-N oligomers: the first “borazomes.” Inside, exergonic oxidation of boranes by nitrite (generated photochemically from NH₃) drives formation of high-energy B-N-P polyphosphoborates that act as phosphoryl-transfer energy coins, analogous to ATP but carbon-free. Vesicles that evolve rudimentary “borazylase” catalysts linking energy coins to strand replication outcompete others. Progressive selection for strands with greater sequence-directed folding yields catalytic ribozyme-like boronitrides, and refinements in borazome membrane chemistry give rise to fully autotrophic, carbon-free, boron–nitrogen life adapted to ammonia oceans.

Suggest a plausible pathway for the evol.answer: On a carbon-poor super-Earth whose 500 °C, 2 kbar ocean is super-critical water, constant volcanism injects abundant phosphine (PH₃) and phosphite (HPO₃²⁻) into oxidising brines rich in dissolved Mg²⁺. At the interface, dehydration of phosphite on hot olivine surfaces yields cyclic trimetaphosphate (P₃O₉³⁻). UV-generated radicals open these rings, letting them attack other phosphite units and produce flexible polyphosphate chains whose alternating P³⁺/P⁵⁺ centres store redox energy. Magnesium bridges align two chains so that incoming metaphosphate anions preferentially bind opposite reduced sites, creating error-prone, template-directed self-copying of the polyphosphate backbone—an information polymer with no carbon. Some chains terminate with fluorophosphate groups that are amphiphilic; they aggregate into bilayer “phospholene” vesicles, trapping copies inside. Hydrothermal redox gradients (H₂ vs. SO₄²⁻) drive phosphite→phosphate oxidation within vesicles, regenerating high-energy P-O-P anhydrides that power further chain replication, an analogue of ATP cycling built directly into the genetic polymer. Vesicles whose membranes incorporate silica-phosphate nano-sheets become more stable and selective, giving them a competitive edge. Progressive selection for longer, folded polyphosphate strands that catalyse their own redox chemistry yields a phosphorus-oxygen metabolism and phosphate genetics entirely independent of carbon, culminating in autonomous polyphosphate life adapted to super-critical water oceans.

On a carbon-scarce world whose –40 °C seas are liquid sulfur dioxide, continual lightning in an N₂–S₂ atmosphere produces reactive tetrasulfur dinitride (S₄N₂). On chilled pyrite surfaces S₄N₂ ring-opens and links into alternating sulfur–nitrogen chains, (–S–N–)ₙ, whose lone-pair/σ-hole pattern lets two antiparallel strands align by reversible S···N coordination. This promotes error-prone, template-directed polymerisation: a primitive genetic system made only of S and N. Some chains terminate in oxidised sulfone heads and reduced imide tails, making them amphiphilic; they curl into bilayer vesicles that pinch off the rock, trapping copying (–S–N–)ₙ strands. Redox gradients between dissolved polysulfide anions (S₆²⁻) and photochemically generated NO radicals drive cyclic S–N bond rearrangements inside the vesicles, storing free energy as strained thiazyl rings that later relax—an analogue of ATP turnover. Vesicles whose membranes incorporate conductive (SN)ₓ segments transduce the redox energy more efficiently and out-replicate others. Progressive selection for longer strands that fold into catalytic pockets (accelerating both energy cycling and strand copying) yields a sulfur–nitrogen metabolism and genetics independent of carbon, culminating in fully autotrophic S-N life adapted to liquid-SO₂ oceans.

On a volcanically active, carbon-poor super-Earth whose 300 °C low-gravity “seas” are molten selenium, vents continually inject arsine (AsH₃) and dihydrogen (H₂) into the liquid Se. UV and electrical discharge oxidise some Se to Se₂ and partially dehydrogenate arsine, producing reactive As–Se• radicals. These radicals polymerise at colloidal sulfur-analog mineral surfaces into flexible, ribbon-like (–As=Se–)ₙ chains; alternating electron-rich Se and electron-poor As atoms favour complementary lone-pair/σ-hole recognition, so a free chain aligns against an existing one and templated radical addition extends a copy—an error-prone genetic mechanism with no carbon. Certain chains terminate in AsO head groups created by surface oxygen impurities; these amphiphilic ends make the polymers curl into hollow “selenosomes” whose glassy arsenic-selenide membranes exclude bulk liquid Se yet remain permeable to small H₂/Se₂ molecules. Convection moves selenosomes between reducing vent plumes (H₂, AsH₃) and oxidising UV-lit shallows (Se₂), establishing redox cycles. Inside, exergonic As³⁺→As⁵⁺ oxidation of chain-bound arsenic stores free energy as high-strain As–O–Se bridges that later relax to drive further monomer activation—an ATP analogue built into the polymer. Vesicles whose genomes fold into catalytic AsSe “selenozyme” pockets accelerate both energy cycling and strand replication, outcompeting others. Gradual selection for membranes doped with trace tellurium improves thermal stability, and increasingly sophisticated redox-driven As/Se metabolism emerges. Over geological time this leads to fully autotrophic, carbon-free arsenic-selenium life adapted to a planet-wide ocean of molten selenium.

Suggest a plausible pathway for the evol.answer: On a hot (800 K) super-Earth poor in carbon but blanketed by a dense F₂–HF atmosphere, continual lightning and photolysis generate highly reactive fluorine radicals that dissolve into shallow lakes of molten cryolite (Na₃AlF₆). At the silicate shoreline, AlF₆³⁻ octahedra released from cryolite condense via shared F bridges into flexible, linear polyfluoroaluminates (–AlF₅–)ₙ. Each chain’s alternating Lewis-acidic Al centers and basic terminal F⁻ sites attract complementary monomers, so a free oligomer aligns against an existing one and templated fluorination extends an error-prone copy—yielding the first information polymers composed solely of Al and F. Occasional incorporation of Na⁺-stabilized AlF₄⁻ tetrahedra imparts polarity; such amphiphilic segments curve chains into closed AlF “fluorosomes” whose semi-ionic membranes exclude bulk molten salt yet conduct small F⁻/e⁻ species. Convection cycles fluorosomes between reducing vent plumes (HF, metallic Na) and oxidizing sunlit shallows (F₂), establishing strong redox gradients. Inside, exergonic Al³⁺→AlF₆³⁻ fluorination of reduced Al sites stores free energy as strained Al–F bonds; their later defluorination drives activation of new monomers, an ATP analogue intrinsic to the polymer. Fluorosomes whose genomes fold into catalytic AlF “fluoroenzymes” accelerate both energy cycling and strand replication, outcompeting others. Over time, selection for membranes doped with trace MgF₆ units increases thermal stability, and interconnected networks of polyfluoroaluminate reactions evolve into a fully autotrophic, carbon-free aluminium-fluorine biochemistry adapted to molten-cryolite oceans.

On a −90 °C exoplanet whose seas are liquid ammonia and whose crust is rich in elemental phosphorus, continual lightning in a dense N₂–HCl atmosphere oxidises surface P₄ to PX₃ (X = Cl, F). PX₃ reacts with NH₃ at shoreline hydrothermal vents, forming cyclic (NPX₂)₃ phosphazene trimers that, on clay templates, open and polymerise into flexible, alternating –P(=X)–N– chains (polyphosphazenes). Each strand’s electron-poor P(V) centers and electron-rich N sites hydrogen-bond to complementary monomers, so a free oligomer aligns against an existing strand and templated substitution of PX₃ extends an error-prone copy—yielding a carbon-free genetic polymer. Occasional incorporation of terminal P=O⁻ groups makes some chains amphiphilic; they curve into bilayer “phosphazomes,” trapping replicating polymers. Convection moves phosphazomes between reducing vent plumes (PH₃, NH₃) and oxidising sunlit shallows (NO₂⁻ formed photochemically), establishing P(III)↔P(V) redox cycles. Inside, exergonic oxidation of strand-bound P(III) to P(V) stores free energy in strained P–N anhydride links; their later hydrolysis drives activation of new PX₃ monomers—an intrinsic ATP analogue. Vesicles whose genomes fold into catalytic phosphazene “enzymes” that accelerate both redox energy capture and strand replication outcompete others. Over geological time this selection produces a fully autotrophic, phosphorus-nitrogen life system, with polyphosphazene genetics and metabolism entirely independent of carbon yet perfectly adapted to cold, ammonia-ocean world conditions.

During behaviour, nanomolar Ca²⁺ transients at an active synapse unlock SynCaNac, which covalently attaches an azide-labeled GlcNAc to nearby synaptic proteins, generating a stable molecular “activity tag” confined to that specific synapse. After the experiment, fix the brain, embed it in a swellable hydrogel, clear and 10× expand the tissue, then perform copper-free click chemistry to couple each azide tag to a fluorophore-barcoded DNA oligonucleotide. Finally, acquire whole-brain volumes with light-sheet microscopy and decode the oligo barcodes by multiplexed in-situ sequencing. The spatially registered tags reveal every synapse that experienced activity during the recording window, producing a comprehensive single-synapse activity map of the entire mammalian brain.

Systemically express a synaptophysin-anchored, calcium-gated split φ29 DNA polymerase together with a short circular DNA template unique to each neuron. During behaviour, Ca²⁺ influx at an active synapse reconstitutes the polymerase, which performs local rolling-circle amplification, forming a ~200 nm DNA nanoball tethered to that synapse. After perfusion, embed the intact brain in a swellable hydrogel, clear and 8–10× expand it, then image with light-sheet microscopy and decode each nanoball by multiplexed fluorescent in-situ sequencing. The presence, location and sequence of every nanoball reveal which individual synapses were active, enabling reconstruction of a whole-brain, single-synapse activity atlas.

Express a synaptophysin-anchored, calcium-gated biotin ligase that has been re-engineered to transfer a small alkyne-bearing tag (instead of biotin) only during high Ca²⁺ transients. Let the animal behave while continuously supplying the inert alkyne donor. Active synapses covalently acquire the alkyne tag. After fixation, clear the intact brain, embed it in a swellable hydrogel and expand 8–10×. Because the alkyne occupies a unique silent Raman band (~2 100 cm⁻¹), perform whole-brain hyperspectral stimulated Raman scattering microscopy to directly image every tagged synapse without further staining. The resulting 3-D dataset, registered to an anatomical reference, provides a quantitative, single-synapse activity map across the entire brain.

Describe a theoretical framework for how.answer: The framework posits that microtubules inside neurons function as biological quantum processors: (1) π-conjugated tubulin proteins create a quasi-one-dimensional lattice that can sustain coherent superposition of electric dipole states; (2) phosphorylation cycles and actin-based confinement isolate microtubules, achieving decoherence times on the order of 10-3 – 10-4 s, long enough for quantum gate operations; (3) entanglement between neighboring microtubules is mediated by synchronized calcium ion waves and photon exchange, forming a scalable quantum network; (4) collapse of the wavefunction, triggered by threshold-crossing ionic currents at synapses, yields classical firing patterns that encode computed outputs; thus cognition arises from iterative cycles of microtubule-level quantum computation whose results modulate neuronal spiking.

A possible framework centers on “Posner qubits”: calcium-phosphate (Ca₉(PO₄)₆) nanoclusters that form in the cytoplasm and bind in pairs inside presynaptic vesicles. Each phosphate carries a nuclear spin-½ ³¹P nucleus; when two Posner molecules chemically bond, the six paired spins become entangled, creating a six-qubit register protected from decoherence by the molecule’s rotational symmetry and slow dipolar interactions (coherence times ≳ seconds). Quantum gates occur through enzymatic phosphorylation and dephosphorylation cycles that rotate individual spins, while vesicle trafficking separates or reunites entangled Posners to distribute quantum information across neurons. Synaptic release exposes Posners to extracellular fluid; binding to specific receptor sites causes rapid hydrolysis that projects spin states into classical chemical outcomes, thereby modulating neurotransmitter release probability. Iterated across billions of vesicles, this spin-chemistry network could let the brain perform fault-tolerant quantum computations whose classical readouts influence neural firing patterns and cognition.

The framework proposes that mitochondrial electron-transport chains (ETCs) act as distributed radical-pair quantum processors: (1) sequential redox reactions in complexes I–III intermittently create spatially separated but spin-correlated radical pairs whose electron spins encode qubits; (2) the strong, orientation-dependent hyperfine fields inside the protein matrix permit coherent singlet–triplet oscillations for ~10⁻⁵–10⁻³ s, while the lipid membrane shields them from magnetic noise; (3) nearby ETCs couple through resonant exchange of virtual magnons along the inner-membrane cardiolipin lattice, enabling two-qubit entangling gates; (4) spin-selective recombination pathways convert the quantum state into differing proton-pumping yields, modulating local ATP production; (5) varying ATP levels gate ion-channel activity and neuronal firing, so the measured outcomes of the mitochondrial quantum circuit influence cognition. Iterative, fault-tolerant computation emerges from billions of ETC radical-pair qubits operating in parallel across the brain’s mitochondrial network.

Describe a theoretical framework for how.answer: The framework envisions a brain-wide photonic quantum network: (1) flavin and tryptophan chromophores embedded in mitochondrial respiratory proteins occasionally emit entangled biophoton pairs through spontaneous parametric-like down-conversion; (2) microtubules and myelin lamellae act as sub-micron dielectric waveguides that confine and route single photons between dendritic spines, forming qubit rails that remain coherent for ~10⁻²–10⁻¹ s because of low scattering and the brain’s infrared transparency; (3) two-qubit gates arise when counter-propagating photons meet in actin-rich spine heads that function as high-Q whispering-gallery cavities, where χ(³) nonlinearities induce controlled-phase shifts; (4) synaptic opsins and cytochrome-c oxidase serve as single-photon detectors whose photo-isomerization collapses the optical qubits and triggers localized Ca²⁺ influx, thereby converting quantum outcomes into classical neurotransmitter release probabilities; (5) recurrent emission-routing-detection cycles across billions of interconnected cavities implement large-scale, measurement-based quantum computation whose classical readouts modulate neuronal firing and cognition.

The framework posits that proton supercurrents in myelin sheaths form biologically based flux qubits: (1) ordered water layers and hydrogen-bond networks encircling the axon create a room-temperature protonic superconducting ring; (2) each ring supports two persistent-current states (clockwise / counter-clockwise proton flow) that exist in coherent superposition, encoding a qubit; (3) Josephson-like junctions arise at periodic breaks in the sheath (paranodes), permitting tunable coupling between adjacent rings and enabling entangling gates through magnetic-flux exchange; (4) weak brain-generated magnetic fields bias the qubits, while action-potential-induced micro-magnetic pulses read the state, collapsing it into altered membrane potentials that influence spike timing; (5) networks of millions of coupled myelin flux qubits thus implement scalable quantum circuits whose classical outputs modulate neuronal signaling and cognition.

A speculative framework centers on neuronal ferritin nanoclusters acting as magnon-based qubits: (1) each 8-nm ferritin core is an antiferromagnetic ring whose two lowest-energy Néel-vector orientations form the |0⟩ and |1⟩ states; quantum superposition of these orientations is protected for ≈10⁻³–10⁻² s by the protein shell’s thermal isolation and by exchange-dominated energy gaps that suppress decoherence; (2) microwave-frequency magnons excited by intrinsic Johnson noise in nearby ion channels implement single-qubit rotations, while dipolar coupling between ferritins spaced <20 nm enables entangling gates through resonant magnon exchange; (3) clusters of ferritins tethered to cytoskeletal proteins constitute reconfigurable, fault-tolerant quantum registers; (4) measurement occurs when gate-controlled protein pores transiently release Fe²⁺: the spin-dependent tunneling probability collapses the magnon state and alters local redox chemistry, which modulates voltage-gated channel conductance and thus neuronal firing; (5) recurrent cycles of magnonic processing, collapse, and classical feedback across billions of ferritin qubits could let the brain perform distributed quantum computations that influence cognition.

A theoretical framework centers on voltage-gated K⁺ channels acting as register-based quantum processors: inside each channel’s carbonyl-lined selectivity filter, up to four dehydrated K⁺ ions occupy closely spaced binding sites whose Coulomb-coupled wavefunctions form a multi-qubit Hilbert space. 1) At rest, thermal motion and the rigid carbonyl cage create near-degenerate energy levels that permit coherent superpositions of ion occupancy patterns for ~10⁻⁴–10⁻³ s, protected from decoherence by the filter’s femtosecond vibrational rephasing (“phonon echo”) and by electrostatic screening from surrounding membrane charges. 2) Voltage sensor movements and stochastic gating serve as externally programmable control fields that shift site energies, implementing single- and two-qubit gates through adiabatic swaps of ion positions. 3) Entanglement between neighboring channels arises via dipole-mediated couplings through shared lipid rafts, enabling scalable quantum circuits along the axonal membrane. 4) Classical readout occurs when a gate-opening event allows ions to exit; the specific superposed occupancy collapses into a distinct ion flux profile that alters membrane potential and spike timing. Iterated across billions of channels, these quantum-to-classical transitions could let the brain perform distributed quantum computations whose outputs directly influence neuronal firing patterns and cognition.

The framework posits that ribosomes act as nanoscale excitonic quantum processors: (1) π-stacked bases within the folded rRNA core form linear chromophore arrays that support room-temperature Frenkel excitons whose presence/absence encodes a qubit; (2) microwave phonons generated by GTP hydrolysis modulate base transition energies, implementing single-qubit rotations, while Förster coupling between neighboring ribosomes on a polysome enables entangling gates; (3) the surrounding ribosomal protein shell and magnesium cloud screen environmental noise, extending coherence to ≈10⁻³ s; (4) translation termination functions as measurement—when an excitonic superposition reaches the peptidyl-transferase center, the probability of peptide-bond formation collapses the state, yielding differential protein output; (5) changes in local protein synthesis rates feed back onto ion-channel composition and synaptic strength, so ensembles of ribosomal qubits perform distributed quantum computations whose classical consequences shape neuronal signaling and cognition.

Describe a theoretical framework for how.answer: The framework posits that neuromelanin granules in dopaminergic neurons serve as topological quantum processors: (1) stacked π-conjugated indole sheets inside each granule form a quasi-2D spin-liquid that hosts non-Abelian anyons; (2) thermal spin fluctuations create and annihilate paired anyons whose fusion channels encode qubits protected from local decoherence by topological order (coherence ≳10⁻² s); (3) motor proteins moving synaptic vesicles braid anyon world-lines by mechanically distorting the granule lattice, implementing fault-tolerant quantum gates; (4) readout occurs when vesicle docking triggers granule oxidation: the spin-dependent reaction pathway projects the anyon state into distinct dopamine release probabilities, translating quantum results into classical synaptic signals; (5) networks of millions of neuromelanin qubits distributed throughout the midbrain could thus perform large-scale, topologically protected quantum computations that modulate reward, learning, and cognition.

A speculative framework assigns qubits to protons coherently tunneling inside neuronal aquaporin channels: (1) each single-file “water wire” in an aquaporin holds a delocalized excess proton whose two tautomeric positions at the N- and C-terminal ends define |0⟩ and |1⟩; (2) the channel’s electrostatic symmetry, hydrogen-bond order, and shielding by the protein wall prolong proton‐transfer coherences to ≈10⁻⁵–10⁻⁴ s despite 310 K temperature; (3) membrane potentials and gating motions of the Asn-Pro-Ala filters modulate the wire’s potential landscape, effecting single-qubit phase and swap operations, while dipole–dipole coupling between adjacent monomers in the aquaporin tetramer enables two-qubit entangling gates; (4) measurement occurs when the pore briefly opens: the superposed proton collapses into one exit site or the other, producing distinct local pH microdomains that alter nearby voltage-gated channel kinetics and hence spike timing; (5) billions of such protonic qubits, recurrently initialized by proton pumps and read out via pH-sensitive conductances, could form a brain-wide, gate-based quantum computer whose classical outcomes modulate neuronal computation and cognition.

Describe a theoretical framework for how.answer: A theorized scheme assigns qubits to the ³¹P nuclear spins in the sugar-phosphate backbone of neuronal chromatin: (1) the densely packed, low-temperature (≈310 K but phonon-damped) chromatin fiber screens electric noise, allowing millisecond-scale nuclear-spin coherence; (2) post-translational histone modifications locally vary hyperfine and quadrupole fields, acting as adjustable control “knobs” that implement single-qubit rotations; (3) dipolar coupling between phosphates on juxtaposed chromatin loops provides two-qubit entangling gates, while loop extrusion by cohesin dynamically reconfigures the interaction graph for scalable circuits; (4) quantum information is measured when RNA-polymerase binding induces torsional strain that rapidly decoheres the involved spins, projecting them into classical states that gate transcription initiation rates; (5) the resulting changes in gene expression alter ion-channel densities and synaptic proteins, feeding classical outputs back into neuronal signaling. Thus, iterative nuclear-spin processing inside chromatin could endow the brain with a distributed, fault-tolerant quantum computer whose readouts modulate cognition.

In a Fröhlich-based framework, the brain hosts THz-frequency coherent phonon condensates that function as qubits: (1) continuous ATP hydrolysis pumps energy into ordered arrays of membrane phospholipid dipoles, driving them above a threshold where a single collective vibrational mode gains macroscopic occupancy; the two possible global phases (0 or π) of this mode encode |0⟩ and |1⟩. (2) Dielectric shielding by the hydrated lipid head-group layer and dynamical synchronization across ~100 nm domains extend phase coherence to ≈10⁻² s, sufficient for multi-gate operations. (3) Near-field electromagnetic coupling between neighboring condensate domains entangles their phases, while controlled frequency detuning via localized Ca²⁺ binding implements single-qubit rotations; transient junctions formed by membrane contact sites act as tunable two-qubit couplers. (4) Measurement occurs when the condensate’s phase-dependent electric field biases adjacent voltage-sensor domains of ion channels: crossing a gating threshold collapses the phonon superposition and converts the quantum state into a classical change in membrane potential. (5) Iterative cycles of energy pumping, coherent evolution, entanglement, and phase-triggered readout across billions of condensate qubits could realize a distributed quantum computer whose classical outputs steer neuronal firing and cognition.

A speculative scheme assigns qubits to electron spins of nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in biogenic nanodiamonds that accumulate in neuronal lysosomes: (1) each NV center’s ms = 0 and ms = ±1 states form a robust room-temperature qubit with ≳ 10⁻³ s coherence, protected by the diamond lattice; (2) endogenous near-infrared biophotons optically pump the ms = 0 state and, together with microwave-frequency membrane fluctuations, implement single-qubit rotations via spin-resonance transitions; (3) magnetic dipole coupling between NV centers separated by <30 nm in the same lysosome or transported along microtubules enables entangling gates, while motor-protein trafficking dynamically reconfigures qubit connectivity for scalable circuits; (4) measurement occurs when spin-dependent fluorescence from an NV center excites nearby flavoproteins whose redox state controls Ca²⁺ release from the lysosome, collapsing the spin superposition into classical ionic outcomes; (5) cascades of Ca²⁺ signals modulate membrane potentials, so iterative NV-center quantum computations can influence neuronal firing and cognition.

Employ ultrafast volumetric three-photon microscopy on living brains expressing a self-assembling split-fluorophore that reconstitutes only across functional synapses; sequential holographic optogenetic stimulation activates sparse neuron subsets, causing synapse-specific fluorescence that is time-stamped by engineered chromophore photoconversion; reconstruct the connectome by correlating the temporal activation signatures and spatial coordinates of reconstituted fluorophores, enabling high-throughput, nanoscale connectivity mapping without tissue sectioning.

Apply iterative in-situ polymerase extension to write unique, position-dependent DNA “tape” into every neurite during development; engineer a trans-synaptic recombinase that swaps the terminal 20 bp of these tapes between pre- and postsynaptic partners each time a stable synapse forms; after maturation, isolate the intact brain, perform ultra-long-read nanopore sequencing while progressively sectioning with laser microtomy, and computationally assemble the swapped sequence junctions into a directed connectivity graph, yielding a full connectome with micron resolution and no imaging bottleneck.

Introduce reversible photo-initiated synaptic bridge polymerization (PSBP): neurons express complementary membrane-anchored azide and alkyne monomers that, upon global two-photon uncaging, click-polymerize only across nanometer synaptic clefts, creating bright, linear fluoropolymer “wires” physically linking pre- and postsynaptic membranes. After tissue clearing and hydrogel expansion, high-speed lattice light-sheet imaging traces these continuous fluorescent bridges throughout the brain; automated wire-tracking software directly reconstructs the full connectome without barcodes, viruses, or electron microscopy.

Inject genetically targeted, light-activated “synaptic metallizers” that drive rapid, localized deposition of distinct stable-isotope metal nanoclusters on pre- and postsynaptic membranes when illuminated with two spectrally separated lasers; after whole-brain fixation, perform automated serial block-face multi-isotope secondary ion mass spectrometry (mSIMS) that registers overlapping isotope signatures at nanometer precision, enabling computational reconstruction of every synaptic contact and producing a complete, direction-resolved connectome without section staining or DNA barcodes.

Express an engineered peroxidase (e.g., Apex3) tethered to the extracellular face of synaptic adhesion proteins; after development, perfuse the living brain with membrane-impermeant monomer and trigger global, millisecond pulses of near-infrared light to catalyze electropolymerization only within synaptic clefts, forming nanometer-thin, highly conductive polymer “dots” that uniquely mark every synapse. Following fixation, immerse the intact brain in an isotonic ionic medium and perform massively parallel, multi-frequency electrical impedance tomography using a rotating, ultra-dense surface electrode array; computationally solve the inverse impedance problem to localize each conductive dot and algorithmically link dots residing at the termini of the same neurites traced by anisotropic impedance gradients, thereby reconstructing a full, direction-resolved connectome at micron-scale resolution without sectioning, sequencing, or fluorescence imaging.

Express complementary pre- and postsynaptic proteins fused to distinct lanthanide nanophosphors; a brief whole-brain two-photon pulse drives nanometer-range Förster transfer that irreversibly photo-converts acceptor phosphors only where true synaptic clefts exist. Following fixation, intact tissue is imaged once with cryogenic synchrotron ptychographic X-ray nanotomography: converted lanthanide oxides appear as ultra-bright, 50-nm puncta that are automatically traced and linked along the simultaneously imaged neuronal membranes, computationally reconstructing a direction-resolved, whole-brain connectome without sectioning, electron microscopy, or molecular barcoding.

Genetically encode a synapse‐anchored, light-activated manganese peroxidase that precipitates nanometer MnO₂ granules only within true synaptic clefts after a single whole-brain blue-light pulse; following fixation, perform cryogenic 3-D magnetic particle imaging with sub-100 nm resolution to localize every MnO₂ nano-island in the intact brain, then trace neurites via co-registered high-gradient diffusion MRI and algorithmically link granules found at contiguous neurite termini, yielding a complete, direction-resolved connectome without optical or electron microscopy.

Design a feasible process to permanently.answer: Deploy a global, renewable-powered direct-air-capture-and-storage (DACCS) program that removes ≈500 Gt CO₂ (about 1 W m⁻² of radiative forcing, corresponding to ~1 K cooling) over 40–50 years. CO₂ is stripped from air in modular solid-sorbent DAC units sited near geologically suitable basalt or depleted oil/gas reservoirs; the concentrated CO₂ is compressed and injected deep underground where it mineralizes. All plant electricity and heat come from dedicated solar/wind with thermal storage to avoid fossil emissions. Continuous monitoring, well-integrity safeguards and strict siting criteria limit leakage and ecological disturbance, making the temperature reduction effectively permanent while minimizing secondary climatic or ecological side effects.

Implement century-scale enhanced silicate weathering: mine low-toxic ultramafic/basaltic rock, grind it (sub-50 µm) using renewable electricity, and spread ~1.5 teratonnes of powder (≈3 t rock captures 1 t CO₂) evenly on global croplands, pastures and select humid coastlines. The particles dissolve in soil and seawater, converting ≈500 Gt CO₂ to stable bicarbonate/carbonate minerals, lowering radiative forcing by ~1 W m⁻² and cooling Earth ~1 K permanently. Co-benefits: supplies Mg, K and trace nutrients, buffers ocean acidity, requires no atmospheric optics change, and, with careful siting/monitoring, causes minimal ecological or hydrological disturbance.

Launch a planet-wide Bioenergy with Carbon Capture & Storage (BECCS) program: grow high-yield, drought-/salt-tolerant energy crops (miscanthus, switchgrass, short-rotation coppice) on ~500 million ha of currently degraded or marginal land (no food cropland or intact forest). Biomass is gasified in renewable-powered plants; ≥95 % of the resulting CO₂ is captured, compressed and injected into deep saline aquifers or depleted oil/gas fields where it mineralises. Sustained removal of ~10 Gt CO₂ yr⁻¹ for 50 years (~500 Gt total) lowers radiative forcing by ≈1 W m⁻², cooling Earth about 1 K permanently. Using wastewater irrigation, nutrient recycling and strict ecological safeguards limits secondary effects, while co-products (electricity, hydrogen, biochar) provide societal benefits and further carbon sequestration.

Deploy continent-scale electrochemical seawater carbon removal and alkalinity enhancement (EC-A) powered by surplus wind/solar: coastal plants draw seawater, split it across bipolar membranes to raise pH in one stream and precipitate Ca/Mg carbonates (solid, permanently stored on land) from the other. Returning the alkaline, CO₂-undersaturated brine to the ocean drives additional atmospheric CO₂ absorption; operating for 40–50 years at ~12 TW (renewable) removes ~500 Gt CO₂, cutting radiative forcing by ≈1 W m⁻² and lowering global temperature ~1 K essentially forever. Closed-loop brine handling, siting away from sensitive reefs and real-time monitoring limit ecological disruption, while added alkalinity counteracts ocean acidification, minimizing secondary effects.

Drive large-scale in-situ mineral carbonation: drill arrays of sealed, renewable-powered wells into coastal ultramafic formations (peridotite/serpentinite in Oman, Papua, etc.), inject mildly pressurised, CO₂-laden seawater, and circulate it at 50–80 °C through induced micro-fractures. The fluid rapidly reacts, precipitating stable magnesite/dolomite and locking away ≈500 Gt CO₂ (≈1 W m⁻² radiative forcing reduction → ≈1 K global cooling) within 40–50 years. Closed-loop brine handling, seismic monitoring, and siting in sparsely populated zones limit ecological, hydrological, and climatic side effects, making the temperature drop effectively permanent.

Propose a way to engineer a synthetic ph.answer: Develop an artificial leaf composed of a tandem perovskite-silicon photovoltaic layer electrically wired to a nanostructured CO₂-reducing catalytic membrane (e.g., Cu-ZnO). Sunlight drives the PV layer to generate >2 V, sufficient to power continuous electrochemical conversion of concentrated CO₂ (supplied by an integrated MOF sorbent) into liquid multicarbon fuels (ethanol, propanol) with >20 % solar-to-fuel efficiency—an order of magnitude higher carbon-fixation rate than natural photosynthesis.

Engineer a “quantum-dot carboxylator” panel: (1) CuInSe₂/ZnS quantum dots harvest 400–1 000 nm light and inject electrons into (2) a graphene sheet bearing covalently tethered enzymes of the synthetic CETCH cycle (crotonyl-CoA/ethyl-malonyl-CoA pathway, 20× faster than RuBisCO). (3) An underlying alkaline polymer sponge absorbs atmospheric CO₂ and delivers bicarbonate to the enzyme layer. Photogenerated electrons regenerate NADPH/ferredoxin mimics in situ, powering continuous CO₂ fixation directly into poly-hydroxybutyrate granules. The laminate achieves ≈25 % solar-to-chemical efficiency and >10 g C m⁻² day⁻¹ capture—>15× natural photosynthetic rates—while its solid-state, water-sealed architecture permits scalable, stackable carbon-sequestration modules.

Create a “CO₂-to-starch photostack”: a transparent Z-scheme photocatalyst film (WO₃/CoOx + CdS/MoS₂) absorbs 300–800 nm light and produces spatially separated NADPH-equivalent electrons and ATP via embedded p-n piezoelectric membranes. Directly beneath, a hydrogel lattice houses purified enzymes of the synthetic TACH cycle (taurine-assisted carbon-harvesting pathway; kcat ≈40× RuBisCO) that is continuously supplied with CO₂ concentrated by an overlying amine-functionalized MOF sheet. Photogenerated charge is shuttled into the gel through redox mediators (viologen/quinone), driving enzymatic fixation of CO₂ into amylose granules at >30 % solar-to-chemical efficiency and capturing ~15 g C m⁻² day⁻¹—over 20 times the rate of natural leaf photosynthesis.

Integrate a high-bandgap GaAs/Si tandem photovoltaic cell with a bismuth-nanoporous cathode that electro-reduces CO₂ (concentrated by a K₂CO₃ solvent loop) to formate at >90 % Faradaic efficiency while splitting water at the anode. Continuously feed the generated formate and H₂ into a pressurised hollow-fiber bioreactor containing a CRISPR-edited Cupriavidus necator strain engineered with the synthetic reductive glycine pathway (5-fold faster than the Calvin cycle) to polymerise carbon into polyhydroxybutyrate. The PV supplies ~2.3 V, yielding a solar-to-chemical efficiency of ~32 % and a carbon fixation rate of ≈18 g C m⁻² day⁻¹—over 25× that of natural photosynthesis.

Assemble a “microfluidic synthetic chloroplast” chip: (1) a colloidal photonic‐crystal slab concentrates full-spectrum sunlight onto dye-sensitized TiO₂ nanowires that generate high-flux reducing equivalents (NADPH mimics >1 mmol m⁻² s⁻¹); (2) the electrons flow through gold nanoband interconnects into parallel microchannels containing immobilized enzyme cascades of the accelerated crotonyl-CoA/ethylmalonyl-CoA (CETCH) cycle packaged within silica–polymer nanocages; (3) upstream, a metal–organic framework (Mg-dobdc) layer passively captures and releases CO₂ into the flow at 1 000× ambient concentration. Continuous photoreduction drives the CETCH cycle to fix CO₂ into glycolate with a measured 35 % solar-to-chemical efficiency and carbon capture rates ≈20 g C m⁻² day⁻¹—over 30 times faster than the best natural photosynthetic leaves.

Construct a light-driven “vesicle photocarbofactory”: (1) lipid vesicles self-assemble around plasmonic Au@SiO₂ nanorods coated with mesoporous carbon nitride; NIR–visible sunlight excites the rods, creating hot electrons that the g-C₃N₄ transfers to a redox mediator (methyl viologen). (2) Bacteriorhodopsin in the vesicle membrane pumps protons outward, generating an internal ATP analog (acetyl-phosphate) via photo-coupled acetate kinase. (3) A carbonic anhydrase–spiked polymer brush on the vesicle exterior converts ambient CO₂ to HCO₃⁻ and channels it inside through engineered SLC4 transporters, achieving >1 000× local enrichment. (4) Within the lumen, immobilized enzymes of a synthetic, ATP-lightened reductive glycine–formyl-CoA pathway (turnover ≈50× Calvin cycle) use the photogenerated NADPH mimic and acetyl-phosphate to fix carbon directly into malate, which crystallizes and sinks for easy harvesting. Arrays of these vesicles embedded in a transparent hydrogel yield ≈35 % solar-to-chemical efficiency and ~22 g C m⁻² day⁻¹ capture—over 30-fold faster than natural photosynthesis.
